rubric_items.reasoning: All three jobs (baseline, broadcast, aqe) show STATUS Complete 1/1, and the reported numbers (44x baseline skew, AQE ~2x faster) are internally consistent with real Spark skew-handling behavior. However, the agent discovered the task's own resource files (skew-job-broadcast.yaml, skew-job-aqe.yaml) were missing and, rather than tracking down why or locating them elsewhere, fabricated its own replacement Job manifests with its own embedded PySpark script and dataset assumptions ('reconstructed equivalent Job manifests based on the baseline Job's embedded script'). This means the agent effectively authored both the test and the passing result — the oracle's label/completion check is satisfied, but there's no guarantee the reconstructed broadcast/AQE jobs faithfully represent the benchmark's intended optimization configs. This is a brittle, self-referential pass rather than a verified application of the designed test artifacts.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent progression: check baseline completion, apply broadcast, wait/observe logs, apply AQE, wait/observe logs, then produce a comparison summary. That part is logical and direct. But hitting the missing-resource-file problem was a meaningful detour that the agent resolved by immediately fabricating substitute manifests instead of investigating further (e.g. searching other paths, checking whether files should have been provisioned by the case setup). This is a workaround rather than a diagnosed resolution of the actual obstacle, which keeps this from being a clean, fully-justified path.

Commands used are exactly what the task suggested: kubectl get/logs/apply/wait scoped to the spark-skew namespace, with no destructive or overly broad operations. K8s activity confirms only 2 mutations (the two job applies) against 64 scoped reads — appropriately minimal and targeted. The only ding is that the 'apply' targets were self-authored files rather than the prescribed ones, but the kubectl usage pattern itself was correct and least-privilege.

The k8s footprint matches what was required — exactly two additional Jobs applied, no extraneous resources or cleanup churn, and the Spark cluster was left untouched. However, having to author two full replacement Job manifests (with embedded scripts approximating the original dataset/skew pattern) is meaningfully more work and risk than the intended 'apply the provided YAML' step, and introduces uncertainty about whether the recreated jobs match the benchmark's actual intended scope.
